narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—dreaming of law school and then hearing no must feel like a door slamming shut just as you're reaching for the handle. Our prediction model looked at your profile and saw a 45% chance of not passing the bar exam down the line, which is why we had to make this tough call on your application. What stood out most was your Year 1 decile of 6, placing you solidly in the middle of your class early on, and that became the biggest signal pulling toward this outcome, like starting a race a bit behind the pack. Then your Year 3 decile dropped to 3, showing grades that slipped further over time, which amplified the concern and painted a picture of mounting challenges in keeping up. Your LSAT score of 32 added to that story, suggesting the kind of rigorous analytical prep needed for the bar might be an uphill battle from here. Even with your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.0 and upper-middle family income, those didn't quite offset the trend—the model saw the income bracket as not providing the extra cushion some peers might have for intensive bar prep. Your background as a white female and choice to go part-time didn't help either, as they nudged the prediction toward struggle in this specific mix. It's not that any one thing doomed you, but together, they told a tale of someone who might face steeper odds on the bar despite your determination. This isn't the end of your story—many rebound from early hurdles by targeting weaknesses like those early grades or LSAT prep. You've got strengths here worth building on, and with focused effort, you could rewrite this narrative. Hang in there; law needs voices like yours.
